q_under1_conscious.text: Is the baby responsive? Are they making purposeful movements, crying, or responding to your voice or touch?

q_under1_not_responsive_breathing.text: Is the baby breathing â€” can you see chest movement or hear breath sounds?

q_under1_breathing.text: How would you describe the baby's breathing?

q_under1_severe_distress.text: Is the baby also: not feeding or drinking, very sleepy or difficult to wake, or has blue discolouration of the lips or tongue?
q_under1_severe_distress.guidance_note: Danger signs in infants under 1 year: inability to feed, lethargy, cyanosis, severe chest indrawing, or grunting are all indicators of severe pneumonia or sepsis â€” both are leading causes of infant mortality in Kenya.

q1to5_conscious.text: Is the child responsive? Can they speak, cry, or make purposeful movements?

q1to5_not_responsive_breathing.text: Is the child breathing â€” can you see chest movement or hear breath sounds?

q1to5_breathing.text: How would you describe the child's breathing?

q1to5_severe_distress.text: Does the child also have: refusal to drink, very high fever, extreme sleepiness, or inability to walk or stand (in a child who normally can)?

q_over5_conscious.text: Is the child responsive and able to speak in full sentences?

q_over5_breathing.text: How would you describe the child's breathing?

q_over5_moderate.text: Is the child able to speak in full sentences, or only a few words at a time?

pre_arrival_instructions: Stay on the line with me.
This is a critical emergency â€” help is on the way.
If the child is not breathing, place them on a firm flat surface.
For a baby: tilt the head back very gently, only to a neutral position.
For a child over 1 year: tilt the head back gently to open the airway.
If the child is not breathing and you are able, start rescue breaths â€” cover their mouth and nose for a baby, or mouth-to-mouth for a child.
If rescue breaths do not work after 1 minute, start chest compressions: 2 fingers in the centre of the chest for a baby, one or two hands for a child.
Stay on the line with me.
This is a serious breathing problem â€” help is on the way.
Keep the child as upright as possible â€” sitting on your lap or propped up.
If oxygen is available, apply it now.
Do not give the child anything to drink if they are struggling to breathe.
If the child becomes less responsive or their lips turn blue, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
Stridor is a sign of a blocked airway â€” this is serious, help is on the way.
Keep the child calm and sitting upright.
If the child has a fever, try to cool them gently â€” remove excess clothing.
If steam is available (a hot shower running in the bathroom), you can sit with the child in the steamy room to help ease the airway.
If the child becomes unable to make sound or their lips turn blue, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
The child has a moderate breathing problem â€” help is on the way.
Keep the child calm and comfortable, sitting upright if possible.
If oxygen is available, apply it now.
Encourage the child to sip small amounts of water if they are willing.
If the child becomes more breathless, their lips turn blue, or they become very sleepy, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
An ambulance is on the way.
Keep the child calm and comfortable.
If the child has a fever, give paracetamol or ibuprofen in the correct dose for their age if available.
Make sure the child drinks small sips of water regularly.
If the breathing gets worse, the child becomes very sleepy, or their lips turn blue, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
An ambulance is on the way.
Keep the child calm and comfortable.
If the child has a fever, give paracetamol or ibuprofen in the correct dose for their age if available.
Encourage the child to drink fluids.
If anything changes â€” the child becomes more breathless, sleepy, or their lips turn blue â€” tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
An ambulance is on the way.
Keep the baby warm and close to you.
If the baby is willing to feed, offer small amounts of breast milk or formula.
Watch the baby closely â€” if they stop feeding, become very sleepy, develop a fever, or their breathing changes, tell me immediately.
